Farn PoLrrrcAL Pnacrrcrs CoN,rN{rssroN
P.O. Ilor 807 o -118 J Srrccr . Sacnurrcnrti. C.{ v-s911,1;3117
(916t lll-.56(r() o [x1 (t)1 6] .lll-0tS6
September 28,2001
Douglas P. Haubert
Burke, Williams & Sorenson, LLP
18301 Von Karman Avenue, Suite 1050
Irvine, Ca 92612-1009
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-196
Dear Mr. Haubert
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Jane Fallon
regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r
QUESTIONS
l. May Ms. Fallon participate in a governmental decision to approve an application for a
sign program?
2. May Ms. Fallon participate in a governmental decision to approve conceptual site
plans for Planning Area 4 of the Hilltop Project?
3. May Ms. Fallon participate in a governmental decision to approve a conditional use
permit application submitted by a member of the Signal Hill Chamber of Commerce, Ms.
Fallon's employer?
4. May Ms. Fallon, as a member of the city's Joint Housing Sub-Committee, participate
in a decision to approve a modification of approved use for a housing projecti
I Govemntent Code sections 8 1000 - 9 l0 14. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-1ti997. of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-01-196
Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
1 and 2. Yes. Ms. Fallon may participate in these decisions if it is not reasonably
foreseeable that a material financial effect on her economic interest will result from these
decisions.
3. Ms. Fallon will not have a disqualifying economic interest in the applicant unless she
has received income of $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
4. Ms. Fallon may participate in this decision provided it is not reasonably foreseeable
that a material financial effect on her economic interest will result from this decision.
FACTS
Signal Hill Planning Commissioner Jane Fallon was recently hired as executive
director by the Signal Hill Chamber of Commerce ("Chamber"), a local non-profit
membership organization. Like other chambers of commerce, it functions generally as a
pro-business advocacy organization that offers members social and business networking
opportunities. Annual Chamber dues range from $50 for a home-based sole
proprietorship to $400 for a business with over 200 employees. The Chamber has gross
annual receipts ofless than $100,000.
As executive director of the Chamber, Ms. Fallon is paid a salary plus a
performance based bonus every other month. The amount of the bonus is determined by
the Chamber's six-member executive board and approved by the Chamber's fifteen-
member board of directors Although not fixed, the amount of the bonus is roughly
equivalent to 30oh of the dues received from new Chamber members. As executive
director, Ms. Fallon is responsible for membership retention and recruitment,
representing the Chamber at various social functions, and preparing financial reports.
Other members of the planning commission are involved in the Chamber.
Current Planning Commissioners Dave Frick and John Ellis each serve on the Chamber's
executive board and the board of directors.
The planning commission will consider projects involving current and prospective
members of the Chamber. For example, in August or September of this year the planning
commission expects to consider an application for approval of a sign program for a
McDonald's fast food restaurant owned by Chamber member Mike Mangione of
Mangione Inc.
The planning commission will also consider large development projects involving
applicants who serve in a leadership capacity with the Chamber. For example, David
Slater, a principal of Signal Hill Petroleum, Inc. serves on the Chamber's board of
directors and executive board. Signal Hill Petroleum, Inc. joined the Chamber prior to
Ms. Fallon being hired as executive director. Signal Hill Petroleum, Inc. is co-developer
and co-applicant for the Hilltop Project, alarge residential development project. The


File No. A-01-196
Page No. 3
Hilltop Project includes five planning areas. A specific plan has been approved for each
of the five planning areas outlining some of the project's details, including the number of
residential units that may be built. The planning areas remain in various stages of
completion. Later this year, it is expected that the planning commission will consider
conceptual site plans for Planning Area 4, which will provide very specific parameters for
each development, including architectural style and home characteristics.
It is also possible that the planning commission may consider an application for a
conditional use permit submitted by a Chamber member whose joining the Chamber
affects Ms. Fallon's bonus. For example, a local business might pay $125.00 to join the
Chamber. It can be expected that a new membership such as this will result in an
increased bonus to Ms. Fallon of approximately $42.00.
Another development project pending in the city is a low and moderate-income
senior housing component of Town Center West. Although the project was already
approved by the Redevelopment Agency, the developer wishes to modify the approved
used from a mix of assisted living units and independent living units to solely
independent living units. Because any modification of the Town Center West project
requires an amendment to an agreement between the City Redevelopment Agency and
the property owner, Town Center West, LLC, all final decisions will be made by the
Redevelopment Agency, not the planning commission. However, the city has created a
Joint Housing Sub-Committee comprised of two members of the Redevelopment Agency
and two members of the planning commission to discuss any modifications to this
project. Ms. Fallon was selected as one of the two planning commission members.
Town Center West, LLC and its principals are not members of the Chamber. LLC has
contracted with Signal Hill Petroleum, Inc. to perform environmental mitigation on the
property where the housing development will be built.
You state that none of these decisions will result in the Chamber being financially
affected.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 8 1001 .)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, partrcipating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests
(Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard analysis
for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given
governmental decision, which is applied below. (Regulation 18700(bXl)-(8) )


File No. A-01-196
Page No. 4
l. 
Is Ms. Fallon a public o{ficial?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100 and 87103;Regulation 18700(bXl).) "Public official" is defined as
"every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government
agency...." (Section 82048.) As a member of the Signal Hill Planning Commission or
member of the Joint Housing Sub-Committee for the Redevelopment Agency, she is a
"public official," and therefore, subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest rules.
2. 
Will Ms. Fallon make or participate in making in a governmental decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or
her agency to any course ofaction, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalfof
his or her agency. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.1 ) A public official "participates
in making a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive review, the offrcial negotiates, advises or
makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision.
(Section 87100; Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her
official position to influence a decision before his or her own agency il for the purpose
of influencing the decision, the official contacts or appears before any member, officer,
employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702j.)
Ms. Fallon clearly will be making or participating in making a governmental
decision if she, as either a planning commissioner or member of the Joint Housing Sub-
Committee, votes on, or negotiates, advises or make recommendations to the
decisionmaker on any of the following:
1) an application for a sign program
2) conceptual site plans for Planning Area 4 of the Hilltop project
3) a conditional use permit application
4) a modification of approved project
3. 
What are Ms. Fallon's economic interests?
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:


File No A-01- 1 96
Page No. 5
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment 2 of $2,000 or more (Section g7103(a);
Regulation 18703. I (a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section g7103(d);
Regulation I 8703. t (b));
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703 2);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prioito the
decision (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703 3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $320 or more within l2 months prior to the decision
(Section 871 03(e); Regulation 18703.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5).
Ms. Fallon has an economic interest in the Chamber arising from her salary as
executive director provided she has received from the Chamber $500 or more in the 12
months prior to a relevant decision. Ms. Fallon may also have an economic interest in a
member of the Chamber if it is determined that the member qualifies as a source of
income to her as a result of a bonus which is akin to "commission income."3 However,
you have indicated that at most she could receive only $120 from any one new member
since 30% (the bonus rate) of the maximum membership fee of $400 totals $120. Absent
any other payments from such a member or other economic interest, Ms. Fallon will not
have an economic interest in Chamber members since income from one source must
r AI indirect investment or interest rneans any investment or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a puuti" oin.lal, or by
a business enti8 or trust in rvhich the official, the ofhcial's immediate family, or theiiagents own directly,
indirectlv' or beneficiallv a 10-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) 
-"ImmediatJ 
family,' is defined
at Section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.
3 Generally, where commission income is received as a result of a transaction, Regulation
18704.3 controls rvho will be treated as the source of the income. "Commission income" is-clefined in
Regulation 18703.3(c)(2) to include only "gross payments received as a result ofservices rendered as a
broker' agent, or oflter salesperson for a specific sale or similar transaction." Consequently, Regulation
18703.3 does not control where a bonus is provided based on the volume of sales as tontrasted with a
paymert received as a commission for a "specific sale or similar transaction." However, in past advice
letters. tlre Commission lus previously addressed several situations involving bonuses. 
'(Sei 
Larsen Advice
Letter. No. I-89-555, andflteAnaforian Advice Letter, No. I-90-312.) These advice letters conclude ftat
certain tYpes of bonuses are analogous to commission payments.


File No. A-01-196
Page No. 6
aggregate to at least $500 within l2 months prior to the decision. Based on the facts you
have provided, income from her bonus for any one Chamber member will not reach this
monetary threshold.
You have provided information regarding only Ms. Fallon's economic interest
resulting from her salary and bonus from the Chamber. For purposes of this letter, we
assume that she has no other economic interest relevant to the governmental decisions
about which you inquire.
4
Is Ms. Fallon's economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the
decisions?
A person, including business entities and sources of income, is directly involved
in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent,
initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request, or is a named party in, or is the subject of, the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency.
(Reg 1870a l(a).)
A person is the subject of a proceeding if it involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract
with, the person. (Reg. 1870a 1@)(2).) If the source of income is not directly involved
in the decision, the source is considered indirectly involved in the decision.
(Reg 18704 1(b).)
In each of the governmental decisions about which you inquired, a person other
than the Chamber was the applicant or subject of the decision. Therefore, th.e Chamber
is only indirectly involved in these decisions.
5. 
Choosing which materiality standards to apply in deciding if there will be a
reasonably forgseeable material financial effect.
This step involves finding the applicable materiality standard set forth in
Commission regulations. (Regulation 18700(b)(5), Regulation 18705, et seq.) For
sources of income which are nonprofit entities indirectly involved in the decision and
which have gross annual receipts of $100,000 or less, the effect of a decision will be
material if'
"(i) the decision will result in an increase or decrease of
the entity's gross annual receipts for a fiscal year in the
amount of $10,000 or more.
"(ii) The decision will cause the entity to incur or avoid
additional expenses or to reduce or eliminate existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more

File No. A-01-196
Page No. 7
"(iii) The decision will result in an increase or decrease
in the value of the entity's assets or liabilities in the amount
of $10,000 or more." (Regulation 18705.3(b)(2)(F) )
Additionally, under the "nexus" rule of Regulation 18705 3(c), any reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of income to a public official is
deemed material if the public official receives or is promised the income to achieve a
goal or purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision.
Consequently, any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the Chamber is deemed
material if any of the decisions would achieve, defeat, aid, or hinder membership
retention and recruitment for the Chamber, representation of the Chamber at social
functions, or financial report preparation. You have not provided sufficient facts for us to
analyze the nexus issue. However, to provide you with additional guidance, we have
enclosed the Wallace Advice Letter, No. I-94-184, which addresses the nexus issue as it
relates to an employee of a city's Chamber of Commerce.
6. 
Is it reasonably foreseeable that the materiality standard will be met?
A material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if it is "substantially likely"
to occur. (Regulation 18706.) You have stated that none of the decisions will have a
financial effect on the Chamber, Ms. Fallon's source of income. Therefore, she does not
have a conflict in Decisions I - 4.
We conclude our analysis here since the facts you have provided do not suggest
that the final two steps of the conflict-of-interest analysis, which are exceptions to the
conflict-of-ihterest rules, are applicable to your situation.
If you have anybther questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By
atalie B
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
l. May Ms. Fallon participate in a governmental decision to approve an application for a
sign program?
2. May Ms. Fallon participate in a governmental decision to approve conceptual site
plans for Planning Area 4 of the Hilltop Project?
3. May Ms. Fallon participate in a governmental decision to approve a conditional use
permit application submitted by a member of the Signal Hill Chamber of Commerce, Ms.
Fallon's employer?
4. May Ms. Fallon, as a member of the city's Joint Housing Sub-Committee, participate
in a decision to approve a modification of approved use for a housing projecti
I Govemntent Code sections 8 1000 - 9 l0 14. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-1ti997. of the California Code of Regulations.
sections.conclusion:
1 and 2. Yes. Ms. Fallon may participate in these decisions if it is not reasonably
foreseeable that a material financial effect on her economic interest will result from these
decisions.
3. Ms. Fallon will not have a disqualifying economic interest in the applicant unless she
has received income of $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
4. Ms. Fallon may participate in this decision provided it is not reasonably foreseeable
that a material financial effect on her economic interest will result from this decision.

used from a mix of assisted living units and independent living units to solely

sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 8 1001 .)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, partrcipating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests
(Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a standard analysis
for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given
governmental decision, which is applied below. (Regulation 18700(bXl)-(8) )
l. 
Is Ms. Fallon a public o{ficial?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100 and 87103;Regulation 18700(bXl).) "Public official" is defined as
"every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government
agency...." (Section 82048.) As a member of the Signal Hill Planning Commission or
member of the Joint Housing Sub-Committee for the Redevelopment Agency, she is a
"public official," and therefore, subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest rules.
2. 
Will Ms. Fallon make or participate in making in a governmental decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or
her agency to any course ofaction, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalfof
his or her agency. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.1 ) A public official "participates
in making a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive review, the offrcial negotiates, advises or
makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision.
(Section 87100; Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her
official position to influence a decision before his or her own agency il for the purpose
of influencing the decision, the official contacts or appears before any member, officer,
employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702j.)
Ms. Fallon clearly will be making or participating in making a governmental

Committee, votes on, or negotiates, advises or make recommendations to the
decisionmaker on any of the following:
1) an application for a sign program

4) a modification of approved project

decisions?
A person, including business entities and sources of income, is directly involved
in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent,
initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request, or is a named party in, or is the subject of, the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency.
(Reg 1870a l(a).)
A person is the subject of a proceeding if it involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract
with, the person. (Reg. 1870a 1@)(2).) If the source of income is not directly involved
in the decision, the source is considered indirectly involved in the decision.
(Reg 18704 1(b).)
In each of the governmental decisions about which you inquired, a person other
than the Chamber was the applicant or subject of the decision. Therefore, th.e Chamber
is only indirectly involved in these decisions.
5. 
Choosing which materiality standards to apply in deciding if there will be a
reasonably forgseeable material financial effect.
This step involves finding the applicable materiality standard set forth in
Commission regulations. (Regulation 18700(b)(5), Regulation 18705, et seq.) For
sources of income which are nonprofit entities indirectly involved in the decision and
which have gross annual receipts of $100,000 or less, the effect of a decision will be
material if'
"(i) the decision will result in an increase or decrease of
the entity's gross annual receipts for a fiscal year in the
amount of $10,000 or more.
"(ii) The decision will cause the entity to incur or avoid
additional expenses or to reduce or eliminate existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more

"(iii) The decision will result in an increase or decrease
in the value of the entity's assets or liabilities in the amount
of $10,000 or more." (Regulation 18705.3(b)(2)(F) )
Additionally, under the "nexus" rule of Regulation 18705 3(c), any reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of income to a public official is
deemed material if the public official receives or is promised the income to achieve a
goal or purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision.
Consequently, any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the Chamber is deemed
material if any of the decisions would achieve, defeat, aid, or hinder membership
retention and recruitment for the Chamber, representation of the Chamber at social
functions, or financial report preparation. You have not provided sufficient facts for us to
analyze the nexus issue. However, to provide you with additional guidance, we have
enclosed the Wallace Advice Letter, No. I-94-184, which addresses the nexus issue as it
relates to an employee of a city's Chamber of Commerce.
6. 
Is it reasonably foreseeable that the materiality standard will be met?
A material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if it is "substantially likely"
to occur. (Regulation 18706.) You have stated that none of the decisions will have a
financial effect on the Chamber, Ms. Fallon's source of income. Therefore, she does not
have a conflict in Decisions I - 4.
We conclude our analysis here since the facts you have provided do not suggest
that the final two steps of the conflict-of-interest analysis, which are exceptions to the
conflict-of-ihterest rules, are applicable to your situation.
If you have anybther questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: l. May Ms. Fallon participate in a governmental decision to approve an application for a
sign program?
2. May Ms. Fallon participate in a governmental decision to approve conceptual site
plans for Planning Area 4 of the Hilltop Project?
3. May Ms. Fallon participate in a governmental decision to approve a conditional use
permit application submitted by a member of the Signal Hill Chamber of Commerce, Ms.
Fallon's employer?
4. May Ms. Fallon, as a member of the city's Joint Housing Sub-Committee, participate
in a decision to approve a modification of approved use for a housing projecti
I Govemntent Code sections 8 1000 - 9 l0 14. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-1ti997. of the California Code of Regulations.

CONCLUSION: 1 and 2. Yes. Ms. Fallon may participate in these decisions if it is not reasonably
foreseeable that a material financial effect on her economic interest will result from these
decisions.
3. Ms. Fallon will not have a disqualifying economic interest in the applicant unless she
has received income of $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
4. Ms. Fallon may participate in this decision provided it is not reasonably foreseeable
that a material financial effect on her economic interest will result from this decision.

Haubert Burke, Williams & Sorenson, LLP 18301 Von Karman Avenue, Suite 1050 Irvine, Ca 92612-1009 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-01-196 Dear Mr. Haubert This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Jane Fallon regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r QUESTIONS l. May Ms. Fallon participate in a governmental decision to approve an application for a sign program? 2. May Ms. Fallon participate in a governmental decision to approve conceptual site plans for Planning Area 4 of the Hilltop Project? 3. May Ms. Fallon participate in a governmental decision to approve a conditional use permit application submitted by a member of the Signal Hill Chamber of Commerce, Ms. Fallon's employer? 4. May Ms. Fallon, as a member of the city's Joint Housing Sub-Committee, participate in a decision to approve a modification of approved use for a housing projecti I Govemntent Code sections 8 1000 - 9 l0 14. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-1ti997. of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-01-196 Page No. 2 CONCLUSIONS 1 and 2. Yes. Ms. Fallon may participate in these decisions if it is not reasonably foreseeable that a material financial effect on her economic interest will result from these decisions. 3. Ms. Fallon will not have a disqualifying economic interest in the applicant unless she has received income of $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. 4. Ms. Fallon may participate in this decision provided it is not reasonably foreseeable that a material financial effect on her economic interest will result from this decision. FACTS Signal Hill Planning Commissioner Jane Fallon was recently hired as executive director by the Signal Hill Chamber of Commerce ("Chamber"), a local non-profit membership organization. Like other chambers of commerce, it functions generally as a pro-business advocacy organization that offers members social and business networking opportunities. Annual Chamber dues range from $50 for a home-based sole proprietorship to $400 for a business with over 200 employees. The Chamber has gross annual receipts ofless than $100,000. As executive director of the Chamber, Ms. Fallon is paid a salary plus a performance based bonus every other month. The amount of the bonus is determined by the Chamber's six-member executive board and approved by the Chamber's fifteen- member board of directors Although not fixed, the amount of the bonus is roughly equivalent to 30oh of the dues received from new Chamber members. As executive director, Ms. Fallon is responsible for membership retention and recruitment, representing the Chamber at various social functions, and preparing financial reports. Other members of the planning commission are involved in the Chamber. Current Planning Commissioners Dave Frick and John Ellis each serve on the Chamber's executive board and the board of directors. The planning commission will consider projects involving current
